source: docs/chess-knowledge.md curriculum; FENs tablebase-verified (Lichess) where ≤7 pieces

lessons.title: King and queen vs king
lessons.keyIdea: Use the queen to box the king in: place her a knight's move away from the enemy king and copy his moves, shrinking his box one square at a time without ever giving check. When he is stuck on the edge, stop moving the queen and walk your own king up. Mate needs both pieces: the queen delivers it only once your king stands two squares away. The one way to ruin it is stalemate, so before every quiet queen move check that the defending king still has a square.

lessons.title: King and rook vs king
lessons.keyIdea: First cut the enemy king off along one rank or file with the rook, a fence he can never cross. March your king up the board behind that fence until the kings face each other. When they stand opposed, check with the rook to force him back a rank, then repeat: this is the ladder. If the enemy king attacks your rook, slide it far along the same rank; the fence holds from any distance.

lessons.title: Two bishops vs king
lessons.keyIdea: The bishops work as a moving wall: side by side they seal off two full diagonals at once. Drive the king toward any corner, you do not need a specific colour, and use your own king to shoulder him the last steps. Advance the wall one bishop at a time so there is never a gap to slip through. Watch for stalemate in the corner itself: leave the king one free square until mate is actually set up.

lessons.title: Bishop and knight vs king
lessons.keyIdea: The hardest basic mate: it only works in a corner the bishop's colour controls, so first decide which corner you are aiming for. The knight and king herd the enemy king along the edge while the bishop closes the escape diagonals. Learn the W-shaped knight path along the edge; it stops the king escaping at the moment he runs for the wrong corner. Be patient: this can take 30 accurate moves, and one slip lets him back to the middle.

lessons.title: Two rooks vs king
lessons.keyIdea: Two rooks mate without any help from your king: one rook cuts the king off, the other checks, and they leapfrog each other rank by rank, the lawnmower. When the king attacks one rook, move that rook far away along its rank; the pattern continues unchanged. Keep the rooks on opposite wings so he can never harass both. This should never take more than a handful of moves.

lessons.title: King and pawn vs king, opposition
lessons.keyIdea: Opposition means the kings stand one square apart and the player NOT to move wins the fight for the squares between them. With your pawn behind your king, step the king forward taking opposition; only advance the pawn once the enemy king has been forced aside. The cardinal sin is pushing the pawn too early: a pawn on the 6th with the wrong king position is the textbook draw. King first, pawn second.
lessons.brief: King and pawn vs king, opposition

lessons.title: Rook-pawn exception
lessons.keyIdea: A rook pawn (a- or h-file) is the great exception: many normally-won positions are drawn because the defending king only needs to reach the corner. As the attacker, get your king to the queening square before his king reaches the corner, or it is a draw no matter how you shuffle. As the defender, run to that corner and stay there; he cannot dig you out without stalemating you.

lessons.title: Square of the pawn
lessons.keyIdea: Draw an imaginary square from the pawn to its queening square, each side as long as the pawn's distance from promotion. If the defending king can step INTO that square on his move, he catches the pawn; if not, it queens. Remember a pawn on its starting square moves two, so count the square from one rank further on. This one picture replaces ten moves of calculation in every pawn race.

lessons.title: Outside passed pawn
lessons.keyIdea: An outside passed pawn is a decoy, not a queening candidate. Push it just far enough that the enemy king must leave the centre to deal with it, then race your own king to the other wing and eat the pawns he left behind. The pawn wins the game by dying at the right moment. Count with the square rule first: the plan only works if his king truly must go.

lessons.title: Distant opposition
lessons.keyIdea: Distant opposition is the same fight as normal opposition, just with three or five squares between the kings. Whoever faces the opponent across an odd gap and is NOT to move holds it. Step toward the key squares keeping the odd gap; the defender mirrors you until he runs out of board. It decides slow king walks long before the kings ever touch.

lessons.title: Lucena position (building a bridge)
lessons.keyIdea: The winning method with rook and pawn: your king sits on the queening square hiding from checks, and the job is to let him out. Put your rook on the 4th rank, walk your king out toward the checks, and when they come, interpose the rook: you have built a bridge. The pawn then queens behind the shelter. If your pawn is not yet on the 7th with your king in front, reach that position first; everything else is preparation for the Lucena.

lessons.title: Philidor position
lessons.keyIdea: The drawing method with rook against rook and pawn: park your rook on your third rank (the rank in front of his pawn's sixth) and wait, keeping his king from crossing. The instant he pushes the pawn to that rank, swing your rook to the back rank and check forever from behind; with the pawn in the way his king has no shelter. Going passive on the back rank too early loses. The timing of the rook swing IS the technique.

lessons.title: Vancura position
lessons.keyIdea: The Vancura draw against a rook pawn on the 6th: your rook attacks the pawn from the side, along the rank, never from behind. When his king walks over to shield the pawn, you check from the side and return to attacking the pawn; he can never make progress because protecting the pawn walks into checks. Keep your king tucked away on the short side. The moment the pawn reaches the 7th this stops working, so set the rook up early.

lessons.title: Rook behind the passed pawn
lessons.keyIdea: Tarrasch's rule: rooks belong BEHIND passed pawns, yours or the opponent's. Behind your own pawn the rook pushes it forward and gains scope with every step; behind his pawn it ties his pieces to defence forever. A rook in front of its own pawn is a tomb: every advance shortens its scope until it must abandon the pawn to get out. Fix the rook placement before pushing anything.

lessons.title: Rook vs a lone pawn
lessons.keyIdea: King and pawn against rook is a race of king positions. The attacker wins by escorting the pawn with the king directly in front of it; the defender draws by cutting the attacking king off or reaching the queening corner in time, where the rook gives itself up for the pawn at the right moment. Calculate the king walks before committing the rook. One tempo decides these.

lessons.title: Opposite-coloured bishops, no pawns
lessons.keyIdea: Opposite-coloured bishops are the great drawing force: even two pawns down, plant your bishop on the diagonal that blocks the pawns and put your king on squares the enemy bishop can never attack. The blockade cannot be broken because his bishop fights on the wrong colour forever. Choose the blockading diagonal early. The attacker's only real try is passed pawns on both wings, too far apart for one blockade.

lessons.title: Opposite-coloured bishops, when pawns win
lessons.keyIdea: To win with opposite-coloured bishops you need two passed pawns separated by at least three files. The defender's king can hold one and his bishop the other, but only while they are close; spread them and one must fall. Advance the pawn his bishop currently stops, forcing the defending king across, then switch your king to the other wing. Improve the king first, push second.

lessons.title: Wrong bishop and rook pawn
lessons.keyIdea: The wrong bishop draw: a rook pawn plus a bishop that does NOT control the queening corner cannot beat a king sitting in that corner. The defender just shuffles in the corner, and stalemate defends everything. Check the corner colour BEFORE trading down into this: one careless exchange turns a win into a dead draw. As the attacker keep another pawn alive; as the defender, sprint for that corner the moment you spot the pattern.

lessons.title: Two connected passers beat a rook
lessons.keyIdea: Connected passed pawns advance as a unit: push the BACK pawn first so they always defend each other, never leaving one loose. Together on the 6th rank they beat a rook outright; that is the threshold to aim for. The defending rook must attack them from behind before they get there. Bring your king over only when a pawn needs an escort for the final step.
lessons.brief: Two connected passers beat a rook

lessons.title: Queen vs rook
lessons.keyIdea: Queen against rook wins by separating the rook from its king. Use checks to drive them apart, or set up zugzwang with the queen a knight's move from the enemy king so any rook move loses it to a fork. The third-rank defence (rook glued beside the king) falls to the same knight's-move zugzwang. Watch for stalemate and desperado rook checks: take the rook only when it is truly free.
